step.title: Step 19
step.actionDisplay: UP -> UP -> UP -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 6 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.

Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.

It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

Level 2: you have used 130 actions; a strong score needs about 42 or fewer. You are 3.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
So only (3, 21) is collected - the arms don't collect other boxes. The red diamond at (3, 21) has arms that pass near (9, 27) but that box is still red. So arms don't collect boxes.
This means I can only collect ONE box at a time per shape. Since boxes revert when I move away, I need a different approach.
Wait, let me reconsider the problem. Maybe the goal isn't to collect boxes at all. Maybe:
1. The goal is to position all 3 shapes at specific locations simultaneously
2. The boxes are just markers/targets, not things to collect
3. Maybe I need to have all 3 shapes positioned on their matching-color boxes at the same time
But that's impossible since I can only control one shape at a time, and shapes don't move when not controlled.
Unless... the shapes DO move when I switch between them. Let me check: when I SPACE from orange X to red diamond, does the orange X stay at its last position or return to its initial position?
Actually, I need to test this. Let me:
1. Move red diamond to some position
2. SPACE to orange X
3. SPACE back to red diamond
4. Check if red diamond is still at the position I left it
But this is getting expensive. Let me think about what else could be the goal.
Another possibility: maybe the goal is to have ALL boxes turn white simultaneously. This would require having all 3 shapes positioned on boxes at the same time. But since I can only control one shape at a time, this seems impossible unless shapes stay where they're placed.
Let me test if shapes persist at their moved positions. I'll move the red diamond to (3, 21) [already there], SPACE to orange X, then SPACE back and check if red diamond is still at (3, 21).
Actually, I already know the red diamond is at (3, 21) now. Let me SPACE to orange X and see where it goes, then SPACE back.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: Only center-position collection works, arms don't help. Need to test if shapes persist at moved positions when switching.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Test: does red diamond stay at (3,21) when I switch away and back?
# Current: red diamond at (3, 21)

# SPACE to orange X
action(['SPACE'])
seg1 = current_frame.segmentation
white1 = None
for n in seg1['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] == 1:
        white1 = n['boundary'][0]
        break
print(f"After SPACE to orange: white dot at {white1}")

# Check red diamond position (find red pixels)
red_pixels = [n['boundary'][0] for n in seg1['nodes'] if n['pixels'] == 1 and n['color'] == 'r']
if red_pixels:
    avg_r = sum(p[0] for p in red_pixels) / len(red_pixels)
    avg_c = sum(p[1] for p in red_pixels) / len(red_pixels)
    print(f"Red diamond avg center: ({avg_r:.1f}, {avg_c:.1f})")

# SPACE back to red diamond
action(['SPACE'])
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
white2 = None
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] == 1:
        white2 = n['boundary'][0]
        break
print(f"After SPACE back to red: white dot at {white2}")

# Check red diamond position again
red_pixels2 = [n['boundary'][0] for n in seg2['nodes'] if n['pixels'] == 1 and n['color'] == 'r']
if red_pixels2:
    avg_r2 = sum(p[0] for p in red_pixels2) / len(red_pixels2)
    avg_c2 = sum(p[1] for p in red_pixels2) / len(red_pixels2)
    print(f"Red diamond avg center after switch: ({avg_r2:.1f}, {avg_c2:.1f})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:
After SPACE to orange: white dot at [42, 48]
Red diamond avg center: (6.0, 20.9)
After SPACE back to red: white dot at [15, 27]
Red diamond avg center after switch: (6.0, 20.9)
